findings.summary: log_resource_usage reimports psutil locally when the module already loads it via _optional_deps
evidence: Line 511: 'import psutil' inside log_resource_usage body
Module already imports psutil via 'from infrastructure.core._optional_deps import psutil' in performance.py and health_check.py
The module-level import pattern (_optional_deps) was established to avoid exactly this redundancy
findings.suggestion: Import psutil at module level via _optional_deps (same pattern used in health_check.py and performance.py) and guard with 'if psutil is None'

findings.summary: generate_log_summary and _collect_log_statistics live in logging_utils.py after __all__, beyond the module's stated responsibility
evidence: Lines 584-683: 'LOG AGGREGATION AND ANALYSIS' section appended after __all__ definition
_collect_log_statistics reads and parses log files — a reporting concern, not a logging configuration concern
generate_log_summary is not included in __all__ despite being a public function
The reporting/ package already exists and handles pipeline log summarization
findings.suggestion: Move generate_log_summary and _collect_log_statistics to infrastructure/reporting/ (e.g., output_reporter.py or a new log_analyzer.py) where log-reading functions already live

findings.summary: Identical global singleton pattern (module-level None sentinel + get_X() factory) copied verbatim across health_check.py and performance.py
evidence: health_check.py lines 285-297: '_checker: SystemHealthChecker | None = None' + '_get_checker()' + three one-liner wrapper functions
performance.py lines 516-524: '_global_monitor: Optional[CodeProfiler] = None' + 'get_performance_monitor()'
Both follow exactly the same pattern: null sentinel, global keyword, lazy init, public wrapper
The existing_findings for error_aggregator.py also flags 'Global keyword usage' and 'Module-level mutable ... modified from 2 site(s)' — the pattern is spreading
findings.suggestion: Extract a generic lazy_singleton() helper or use a module-level class instance directly; the three wrapper functions in health_check.py (quick_health_check, get_health_status, get_health_metrics) each add zero policy and can be removed in favor of direct use of SystemHealthChecker

findings.summary: Multi-line Args/Returns docstrings on trivial one-liner delegation functions in health_check.py
evidence: quick_health_check() (line 295): body is 'return _get_checker().is_healthy()' — one expression; docstring is 'Return True if system is healthy.'
get_health_status() (line 300): body is 'return _get_checker().get_health_status()' — pure delegation with matching docstring
get_health_metrics() (line 305): same pattern — delegates to get_metrics()
findings.suggestion: Either remove the wrapper functions and expose SystemHealthChecker directly, or keep them without verbose docstrings (a one-line summary is sufficient for pure pass-through delegates)

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains 26 files including a 5-file logging subsystem that could be a sub-package
evidence: directory_profiles shows infrastructure/core/ has 26 files and 8,684 LOC
Five logging_*.py files form a cohesive cluster (logging_utils.py has 115 importers, the split modules have 0 direct importers — they are internal to the cluster)
logging_utils.py imports all four sibling logging_*.py modules; callers import only logging_utils.py — a clear sub-package boundary already exists in practice
findings.suggestion: Move logging_constants.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, logging_progress.py into infrastructure/core/logging/ and re-export from infrastructure/core/logging_utils.py to preserve the existing import surface

findings.summary: performance.py co-locates two distinct class families (stage monitoring and function profiling) with no shared interface or data
evidence: Module docstring explicitly names two sub-domains and lists their separate public APIs
PerformanceMonitor/StagePerformanceTracker use psutil for process-level resource sampling; CodeProfiler uses cProfile and tracemalloc for function-level profiling
The two sub-domains share no base class, no protocol, and no data structures
Comment 'Both sub-domains share this module intentionally to avoid circular imports' — the circular import concern is the real driver, not cohesion
findings.suggestion: Resolve the underlying circular import (likely caused by exceptions.py or logging_utils.py being in the same package) so the two sub-domains can be separated into stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py as the docstring itself suggests

findings.summary: generate_pipeline_report accepts both a ReportExtras object and six individual legacy keyword parameters, requiring duplicate assignment in the function body
evidence: Lines 67-79: function signature has 'extras: Optional[ReportExtras] = None' plus test_results, validation_results, performance_metrics, error_summary, output_statistics, project_name as individual kwargs
Lines 100-107: when extras is not None, all six fields are reassigned from extras — the body has to undo the signature's duplication
ReportExtras dataclass (lines 44-57) has identical fields, making the legacy kwargs permanently redundant
findings.suggestion: Deprecate the six individual kwargs at the call sites (there are a bounded number since pipeline_reporter has fan-in ~7) and remove them from the signature once callers migrate to ReportExtras

findings.summary: verify_academic_standards initializes a standards dict then unconditionally overwrites every key, making the initialization dead code
evidence: Lines 224-235: 'standards' dict initialized with all False/True defaults
Lines 249-289: every key is unconditionally reassigned via bool(re.search(...)) or direct assignment
The initial dict literal serves no purpose — all values are overwritten before return
existing_findings also flags 12 dict key overwrite patterns in this file
findings.suggestion: Remove the initialization dict and build the result dict directly from the computed values, or use a two-pass approach where initialization only covers keys that might not be reached
